Article.ArticleTitle: Multi-factor interactions in myosin gelation: Unraveling the ultrasound-NaCl synergy through structural and thermal properties perspectives.

Abstract.AbstractText: This study investigated the synergistic effects of high-intensity ultrasound (HIU, 20 kHz, 13.4 W/cm2, 3 min) and varying NaCl concentrations (0, 0.3, 0.6, and 0.9 mol/L) on the structural modifications of myosin, its thermal stability, and heat-induced gel properties. By integrating multi-scale analytical techniques (FT-IR, UV, DSC, LF-NMR, TPA, and SEM), we revealed a salt-dependent dual role of HIU. At low NaCl concentrations (≤0.6 mol/L), HIU disrupted myosin aggregates through cavitation, reducing α-helix content and exposing hydrophobic residues, thereby impairing thermal stability and resulting in coarse, heterogeneous gel networks with weakened water-holding capacity. Conversely, under high sodium chloride (0.9 mol/L), ionic shielding alleviated the structural damage induced by HIU, preserved the integrity of the α-helix, and mitigated thermal stability impairment. This synergy promoted ordered protein hydration and homogeneous gel formation, achieving superior water-holding capacity (WHC) and mechanical strength. The study establishes a mechanistic link between myosin conformational dynamics under HIU-NaCl interactions and macroscopic gel performance, providing a novel strategy to tailor textural properties in low-salt meat products. These findings present new perspectives on protein-ultrasound interactions, laying the groundwork for innovative approaches (e.g., combined physical treatments or alternative salt formulations) to optimize low-salt meat processing.
